name: Apu
description: Apu are the spirits of mountains in Incan mythology. They are considered to be powerful deities who watch over the people and the land. Each significant mountain has its own Apu, and these spirits are often associated with specific powers and responsibilities. They are not necessarily benevolent, and can be capricious, demanding respect and offerings. Apu can influence the weather, control the fertility of the land, and offer protection to those who live in their vicinity. They are often consulted for guidance and their favor is sought through rituals and sacrifices. The Apu are deeply intertwined with the Incan worldview, representing the sacredness of the natural world and the interconnectedness of all things. To disrespect an Apu could bring misfortune, such as drought, famine, or landslides. Therefore, the Incan people treated the mountains with reverence and performed ceremonies to appease these powerful spirits. They are seen as living entities possessing agency and influence over the lives of those who dwell in their shadow. The most important Apus were often associated with specific regions and played a crucial role in the political and social structure of the Inca empire. Their perceived power was used to legitimize authority and reinforce social hierarchies. The continued reverence for Apu is still present in some Andean communities today, demonstrating the enduring legacy of Incan beliefs.
subtitle: Mountain Spirits of the Inca

keyMyths.title: The Wrath of Apu Ausangate
keyMyths.description: A legend tells of Apu Ausangate, angered by the disrespect of villagers, causing a devastating flood that wiped out their crops and homes. Only through sincere apologies and offerings were they able to appease the mountain spirit.

keyMyths.title: Apu and the Lost Child
keyMyths.description: A story recounts how a child lost in the mountains was guided back to safety by the benevolent spirit of the local Apu. The child was protected from the elements and led to a familiar path.

keyMyths.title: The Offering to Apu Salkantay
keyMyths.description: Before embarking on a dangerous journey, Incan travelers would offer coca leaves and chicha to Apu Salkantay. This was done to ensure safe passage and protection from harm during their travels.

sources.author: Bauer, Brian S.
